1:
"Put them in mind to be subject to principalities and powers, to obey magistrates, to be ready to every good work," The witnesses note the charge to submit to civil authority and be ready for good works. Gill notes "principalities and powers" are not angels but men in high places, the higher powers ordained of God, distinguished by Peter into the king as supreme and the governors under him. The witnesses note Paul charging that the believers be put in mind to be subject to principalities and powers, to obey magistrates, and to be ready to every good work; the duty of Christian submission to government and readiness for good — that Paul, turning to the believers' conduct in the world, bids Titus "put them in mind" (remind them of a known duty) to "be subject to principalities and powers" (the civil rulers and authorities ordained of God), "to obey magistrates" (yielding lawful obedience to those in office), and "to be ready to every good work" (prompt and willing for all that is good) — so that Christians, far from being seditious, are to be model subjects, obedient to government and forward in every good deed; to be subject to principalities and powers, to obey magistrates. The witnesses note put them in mind to be subject to principalities and powers. The witnesses note to obey magistrates, to be ready to every good work.

The witnesses note the duty of submission to civil authority and readiness for good — Christians, far from seditious, are to be model subjects, obedient and forward in every good deed.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of submission to government. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are to obey the civil powers ordained of God and be ready for every good work. The witnesses note the put in mind: "put them in mind" — Gill notes remind them of a known duty; the put in mind; the remind; the a known duty; the the believers; the recall. The expositors observe the principalities and powers: "to be subject to principalities and powers" — civil rulers ordained of God, not angels; the principalities and powers; the civil rulers; the ordained; the not angels; the the higher powers. The witnesses press the obey magistrates: "to obey magistrates" — yielding lawful obedience to those in office; the obey magistrates; the lawful obedience; the in office; the the rulers; the obey. The expositors draw the lesson on the every good work: "to be ready to every good work" — prompt and willing for all good; the every good work; the prompt; the willing; the all good; the ready. The witnesses observe the model subjects: Christians are to be model subjects, not seditious; the model subjects; the not seditious; the obedient; the the believers; the good. The expositors note the ordained of God: the powers are ordained of God; the ordained of God; the the powers; the the rulers; the from God; the authority. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the put them in mind to be subject to principalities and powers, the to obey magistrates to be ready to every good work, and the duty of submission. The verse thus records the duty of submission to government — teaching that believers are to obey the civil powers ordained of God and be ready for every good work.
2:
"To speak evil of no man, to be no brawlers, but gentle, shewing all meekness unto all men." The witnesses note the charge to gentle, peaceable speech and conduct toward all. Gill notes they are to speak evil of no man — not of one another, nor of the men of the world, nor of magistrates and those in authority, as the false teachers did, to the prejudice of their names and characters. The witnesses note Paul charging that they speak evil of no man, be no brawlers, but gentle, showing all meekness unto all men; the peaceable, gentle, and meek temper required toward everyone — that Paul completes the believers' social duty with a charge to a gracious spirit toward all: "to speak evil of no man" (no slandering or reviling of anyone, ruler or neighbour), "to be no brawlers" (not contentious or quarrelsome), "but gentle" (mild and equitable, yielding rather than fierce), "shewing all meekness unto all men" (a humble, patient, gentle temper toward every person, friend or foe) — so that the Christian is to be marked by a peaceable and gentle disposition in all his dealings; to be no brawlers, but gentle, shewing all meekness unto all men. The witnesses note to speak evil of no man, to be no brawlers. The witnesses note but gentle, shewing all meekness unto all men.

The witnesses note the charge to gentle, peaceable conduct — speaking evil of no man, no brawlers, but showing all meekness to all; a gracious temper toward everyone, friend or foe.

So the witnesses make the verse the peaceable, gentle temper toward all. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Christian is to slander none, quarrel with none, but show meekness to all men. The witnesses note the speak evil of none: "to speak evil of no man" — Gill notes no slandering of anyone, ruler or neighbour; the speak evil of none; the no slander; the anyone; the ruler; the neighbour. The expositors observe the no brawlers: "to be no brawlers" — not contentious or quarrelsome; the no brawlers; the not contentious; the not quarrelsome; the peaceable; the calm. The witnesses press the gentle: "but gentle" — mild and equitable, yielding; the gentle; the mild; the equitable; the yielding; the not fierce. The expositors draw the lesson on the all meekness: "shewing all meekness unto all men" — a humble, patient, gentle temper toward all; the all meekness; the humble; the patient; the gentle; the toward all. The witnesses observe the friend or foe: meekness toward every person, friend or foe; the friend or foe; the every person; the all men; the toward all; the gracious. The expositors note the peaceable disposition: marked by a peaceable, gentle disposition; the peaceable disposition; the gentle; the all dealings; the temper; the gracious. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to speak evil of no man to be no brawlers, the but gentle shewing all meekness unto all men, and the gentle temper. The verse thus records the peaceable, gentle temper toward all — teaching that the Christian is to slander none, quarrel with none, but show meekness to all men.
3:
"For we ourselves also were sometimes foolish, disobedient, deceived, serving divers lusts and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful, and hating one another." The witnesses note the remembrance of the believers' own former sinful state. Gill notes nothing has a greater tendency to promote humility and check pride in the saints than to reflect on their past state — what they themselves once were; and this is a reason why even evil magistrates should be obeyed and no man spoken evil of. The witnesses note Paul reminding that we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, deceived, serving lusts and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful and hating one another; the humbling memory of what we once were as the ground of gentleness — that Paul gives the reason for the gentleness toward all just enjoined: the remembrance of our own past — "for we ourselves also were sometimes" all this — "foolish" (without spiritual understanding), "disobedient" (to God), "deceived" (led astray by error), "serving divers lusts and pleasures" (slaves to our appetites), "living in malice and envy, hateful, and hating one another" (full of ill-will and mutual hatred) — so that, remembering we were once no better than the worst we now meet, we have every reason to deal gently with others, our own former state forbidding all pride; for we ourselves also were sometimes foolish, disobedient, deceived. The witnesses note for we ourselves also were sometimes foolish, disobedient, deceived. The witnesses note living in malice and envy, hateful, and hating one another.

The witnesses note the humbling memory of what we once were — foolish, disobedient, slaves to lust, full of malice — the ground of gentleness, our former state forbidding all pride.

So the witnesses make the verse the humbling memory of our former state. The lesson the expositors draw is that, remembering we were once no better than the worst, we have reason to deal gently with all. The witnesses note the we also were: "for we ourselves also were sometimes foolish" — Gill notes reflecting on our past checks pride; the we also were; the our past; the checks pride; the once; the humility. The expositors observe the foolish and disobedient: "disobedient, deceived" — without understanding, disobedient to God, led astray; the foolish; the disobedient; the deceived; the no understanding; the astray. The witnesses press the serving lusts: "serving divers lusts and pleasures" — slaves to our appetites; the serving lusts; the divers pleasures; the slaves; the appetites; the bondage. The expositors draw the lesson on the malice and envy: "living in malice and envy" — full of ill-will and envy; the malice and envy; the ill-will; the envy; the spite; the hatred. The witnesses observe the hating one another: "hateful, and hating one another" — hateful and mutually hating; the hating one another; the hateful; the mutual hatred; the ill-will; the hating. The expositors note the ground of gentleness: this memory is the ground of gentleness toward all; the ground of gentleness; the gentleness; the toward all; the forbids pride; the humble. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for we ourselves also were sometimes foolish disobedient deceived, the living in malice and envy hateful and hating one another, and the humbling memory. The verse thus records the humbling memory of our former state — teaching that, remembering we were once no better than the worst, we have reason to deal gently with all.
4:
"But after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared," The witnesses note the appearing of God's kindness and love as the turning point. Gill notes this was "after" all that series and course of wickedness, notwithstanding all that foolishness and disobedience; or "when" all this was, amid all this iniquity, when these persons were in the full career of sin, and so had done no preparatory works. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared, the great change came; the free kindness of God breaking in upon our sin — that Paul, having drawn the dark picture of what we were, sets against it the bright "but" of grace: "after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared" — when, amid all that wickedness, with no preparatory works on our part, the "kindness" (benignity) and "love toward man" (philanthropy, God's love to mankind) of God our Saviour shone forth in the gospel — so that the whole change in us sprang not from anything in us but from the free, spontaneous kindness of God, breaking in upon our sin; the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared. The witnesses note but after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour. The witnesses note toward man appeared.

The witnesses note the bright "but" of grace — the kindness and love of God appearing, amid all our wickedness and with no preparatory works, breaking in upon our sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the free kindness of God breaking in. The lesson the expositors draw is that the change sprang not from us but from the free kindness and love of God appearing in the gospel. The witnesses note the after that: "but after that" — Gill notes after all our wickedness, amid our sin; the after that; the after wickedness; the amid sin; the the turning point; the but. The expositors observe the no preparatory works: with no preparatory works on our part; the no preparatory works; the nothing in us; the no merit; the in full sin; the free. The witnesses press the kindness: "the kindness... of God our Saviour" — the benignity of God our Saviour; the kindness; the benignity; the of God; the our Saviour; the gracious. The expositors draw the lesson on the love toward man: "and love of God our Saviour toward man" — God's love to mankind, his philanthropy; the love toward man; the philanthropy; the to mankind; the God's love; the appeared. The witnesses observe the appeared: "appeared" — shone forth in the gospel; the appeared; the shone forth; the the gospel; the made manifest; the broke in. The expositors note the not from us: the change sprang not from us but from God's free kindness; the not from us; the free kindness; the God's; the spontaneous; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour, the toward man appeared, and the kindness breaking in. The verse thus records the free kindness of God breaking in — teaching that the change sprang not from us but from the free kindness and love of God appearing in the gospel.
5:
"Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us, by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost;" The witnesses note that salvation is by God's mercy, not our works. Gill notes "works of righteousness" are works done according to a righteous law, in obedience to it, and in a righteous manner; salvation is denied to be brought about by any works, even the best works of men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy God saved us, by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost; the salvation that is wholly of mercy, not works, wrought by the renewing Spirit — that Paul states the ground of salvation with the fullest clearness: it is "not by works of righteousness which we have done" (not by any deeds of ours, however good, as cause or condition) "but according to his mercy he saved us" (the sole moving cause being God's free mercy) — and he names the means by which that mercy works in us: "the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost" — the inward cleansing and new birth, the renewing work of the Spirit who makes us new creatures; the witnesses note the debate whether "the washing of regeneration" points to baptism as a sign, or chiefly to the inward renewing of the Spirit which it signifies, all agreeing the saving work is the Spirit's; according to his mercy he saved us, by the washing of regeneration. The witnesses note not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us. The witnesses note by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost.

The witnesses note salvation wholly of mercy, not works — God saving us by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost; the saving work the Spirit's, not our deeds.

So the witnesses make the verse salvation by mercy through the renewing Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that God saves not by our works but by his mercy, through the regenerating and renewing work of the Spirit. The witnesses note the not by works: "not by works of righteousness which we have done" — Gill notes salvation not by any works of ours; the not by works; the not our deeds; the not righteousness; the not cause; the not condition. The expositors observe the his mercy: "but according to his mercy he saved us" — the sole moving cause is God's free mercy; the his mercy; the the moving cause; the free; the saved us; the mercy. The witnesses press the washing of regeneration: "by the washing of regeneration" — the inward cleansing and new birth; the washing of regeneration; the cleansing; the new birth; the inward; the renewed. The expositors draw the lesson on the renewing Spirit: "and renewing of the Holy Ghost" — the renewing work of the Spirit making us new; the renewing Spirit; the the Holy Ghost; the new creatures; the renewed; the the Spirit. The witnesses present the debate on "washing": whether "the washing of regeneration" points to baptism as a sign, or chiefly to the inward renewing the Spirit works which baptism signifies; all agree the saving work is the Spirit's, not the water's. The expositors note the wholly of grace: salvation is wholly of grace, not works; the wholly of grace; the mercy; the the Spirit; the grace. The verse thus records salvation by mercy through the renewing Spirit — teaching that God saves not by our works but by his mercy, through the regenerating Spirit.
6:
"Which he shed on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Saviour;" The witnesses note the abundant outpouring of the Spirit through Christ. Gill notes this means either the love of God shed abroad in the hearts of the regenerate, or the grace, comparable to water, plentifully shed where sin had abounded, or rather the Holy Spirit himself, with his gifts and graces, shed on them abundantly. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God shed the Holy Spirit on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Saviour; the rich outpouring of the renewing Spirit, given through Christ — that Paul, having named the renewing of the Holy Ghost (v.5), tells how richly that Spirit is given: God "shed" (poured out) the Spirit "on us abundantly" (richly, plenteously, not in scant measure) — and that "through Jesus Christ our Saviour," for it is by and through the mediation of Christ that the Spirit is bestowed on his people — so that the very fountain of the new life, the Holy Spirit, is given not sparingly but in rich abundance, and that purchased and channelled through the Saviour; which he shed on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Saviour. The witnesses note which he shed on us abundantly. The witnesses note through Jesus Christ our Saviour.

The witnesses note the rich outpouring of the renewing Spirit — shed on us abundantly through Jesus Christ; the fountain of new life given not sparingly but in rich abundance.

So the witnesses make the verse the abundant outpouring of the Spirit through Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the renewing Spirit is poured out richly on God's people through the mediation of Christ. The witnesses note the shed: "which he shed on us" — Gill notes God poured out the Spirit on us; the shed; the poured out; the the Spirit; the on us; the given. The expositors observe the abundantly: "abundantly" — richly, plenteously, not scant; the abundantly; the richly; the plenteously; the not scant; the full. The witnesses press the the Spirit: the Spirit himself, with his gifts and graces, shed; the the Spirit; the his gifts; the his graces; the shed; the poured. The expositors draw the lesson on the through Christ: "through Jesus Christ our Saviour" — by and through the mediation of Christ; the through Christ; the the mediation; the our Saviour; the channelled; the bestowed. The witnesses observe the fountain of life: the Spirit, the fountain of new life, given richly; the fountain of life; the the Spirit; the new life; the richly; the given. The expositors note the purchased by Christ: the Spirit purchased and channelled through the Saviour; the purchased by Christ; the channelled; the the Saviour; the through him; the given. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which he shed on us abundantly, the through Jesus Christ our Saviour, and the outpouring of the Spirit. The verse thus records the abundant outpouring of the Spirit through Christ — teaching that the renewing Spirit is poured out richly on God's people through the mediation of Christ.
7:
"That being justified by his grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life." The witnesses note justification by grace and the heirship of eternal life. Gill notes this is another way and means, as well as regeneration, by which God saves his people; for he saves no unjustified ones — no unrighteous persons shall inherit the kingdom of heaven, but those whom God saves he justifies. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, being justified by his grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life; the justification by grace that makes us heirs of eternal life — that Paul names the end and fruit of all this saving work: "being justified by his grace" (accounted righteous before God freely, not by our works but by his grace through the righteousness of Christ), the purpose was that "we should be made heirs" (adopted into God's family as sons and heirs) "according to the hope of eternal life" — heirs of the very glory hoped for; so that the same free grace that regenerates and justifies also makes the believer an heir of eternal life, the whole of salvation, first to last, being of grace; being justified by his grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life. The witnesses note that being justified by his grace. The witnesses note we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life.

The witnesses note justification by grace making us heirs of eternal life — accounted righteous freely, not by works, and adopted as heirs of the glory hoped for.

So the witnesses make the verse justification by grace and the heirship of life. The lesson the expositors draw is that the same free grace that saves justifies the believer and makes him an heir of eternal life. The witnesses note the justified: "that being justified by his grace" — Gill notes accounted righteous freely by grace, not works; the justified; the by grace; the freely; the not works; the righteous. The expositors observe the Christ's righteousness: justified by the righteousness of Christ; the Christ's righteousness; the imputed; the righteous; the by grace; the justified. The witnesses press the made heirs: "we should be made heirs" — adopted as sons and heirs; the made heirs; the adopted; the sons; the the family; the heirs. The expositors draw the lesson on the hope of life: "according to the hope of eternal life" — heirs of the glory hoped for; the hope of life; the eternal life; the the glory; the hoped for; the heirs. The witnesses observe the saves no unjustified: God saves no unjustified ones; the saves no unjustified; the no unrighteous; the inherit; the justified; the saved. The expositors note the all of grace: the whole of salvation, first to last, is of grace; the all of grace; the first to last; the the whole; the salvation; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that being justified by his grace, the we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life, and the heirship of life. The verse thus records justification by grace and the heirship of life — teaching that the same free grace that saves justifies the believer and makes him an heir of eternal life.
8:
"This is a faithful saying, and these things I will that thou affirm constantly, that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works. These things are good and profitable unto men." The witnesses note the charge to affirm these truths so that believers maintain good works. Chrysostom notes that, having spoken of God's love to man and what he has done for us, Paul adds these things to be affirmed constantly, that those who have believed might be careful to maintain good works — discoursing of these mercies to exhort to almsgiving and every good deed. The witnesses note Paul declaring it a faithful saying, to be affirmed constantly, that believers be careful to maintain good works; the constant affirming of saving grace that it may produce good works — that Paul seals the whole passage on salvation by grace as "a faithful saying" (certainly true and to be relied on), and charges Titus to "affirm constantly" (keep insisting on) these truths, and that to a practical end: "that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works" — that the doctrine of free grace, far from making men careless, might rouse the justified to a diligent, studious pursuit of good works, out of gratitude; "these things are good and profitable unto men" — so that the preaching of grace and the pressing of good works go together; that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works. The witnesses note this is a faithful saying. The witnesses note that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works.

The witnesses note the constant affirming of saving grace — that believers be careful to maintain good works; free grace, far from making careless, rousing the justified to good.

So the witnesses make the verse the affirming of grace that produces good works. The lesson the expositors draw is that the doctrine of free grace is to be constantly pressed, that the justified might be diligent in good works. The witnesses note the faithful saying: "this is a faithful saying" — Paul seals the doctrine of grace as certainly true; the faithful saying; the certainly true; the reliable; the the doctrine; the sealed. The expositors observe the affirm constantly: "these things I will that thou affirm constantly" — keep insisting on these truths; the affirm constantly; the insisting; the keep; the these truths; the press. The witnesses press the maintain good works: "that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works" — that believers be diligent in good works; the maintain good works; the careful; the diligent; the believers; the good. The expositors draw the lesson on Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes grace is discoursed to exhort to good deeds; the Chrysostom; the grace; the exhort; the good deeds; the almsgiving. The witnesses observe the not careless: free grace, far from making careless, rouses to good works; the not careless; the free grace; the rouses; the gratitude; the good. The expositors note the good and profitable: "these things are good and profitable unto men" — these truths are good and profitable; the good and profitable; the these truths; the unto men; the useful; the good. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is a faithful saying, the that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works, and the grace that produces good works. The verse thus records the affirming of grace that produces good works — teaching that the doctrine of free grace is to be constantly pressed, that the justified might be diligent in good works.
9:
"But avoid foolish questions, and genealogies, and contentions, and strivings about the law; for they are unprofitable and vain." The witnesses note the charge to avoid foolish disputes and strivings about the law. Gill notes the "foolish questions" are such as were started in the schools of the Jews. Matthew Henry notes this is the last thing in the matter of the epistle — what Titus should avoid in teaching, with directions how to deal with a heretic. The witnesses note Paul charging that Titus avoid foolish questions, genealogies, contentions, and strivings about the law, as unprofitable and vain; the rejection of fruitless controversy in favour of edifying truth — that Paul, having pressed the maintaining of good works, warns against their great hindrance and the opposite of profitable teaching: "foolish questions" (silly speculations), "genealogies" (idle Jewish tracings of descent), "contentions, and strivings about the law" (wranglings over the ceremonial law) — all of which "are unprofitable and vain," yielding no good, building up no one — so that Titus is to turn from barren controversy to the sound, edifying truths that produce godliness; for they are unprofitable and vain. The witnesses note avoid foolish questions, and genealogies, and contentions, and strivings about the law. The witnesses note for they are unprofitable and vain.

The witnesses note the charge to avoid foolish questions and strivings about the law — unprofitable and vain; Titus turning from barren controversy to edifying truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejection of fruitless controversy. The lesson the expositors draw is that Titus is to shun idle disputes about the law and turn to edifying truth. The witnesses note the foolish questions: "avoid foolish questions" — Gill notes silly speculations from the Jewish schools; the foolish questions; the silly; the speculations; the the Jewish schools; the idle. The expositors observe the genealogies: "and genealogies" — idle tracings of descent; the genealogies; the descent; the tracings; the idle; the Jewish. The witnesses press the contentions: "and contentions, and strivings about the law" — wranglings over the ceremonial law; the contentions; the strivings about the law; the wranglings; the the ceremonial law; the disputes. The expositors draw the lesson on the unprofitable: "for they are unprofitable and vain" — yielding no good, building up none; the unprofitable; the vain; the no good; the build up none; the barren. The witnesses observe the hindrance: these hinder the maintaining of good works; the the hindrance; the good works; the the opposite; the barren; the hinder. The expositors note the edifying truth: Titus turns to sound, edifying truths; the edifying truth; the sound; the godliness; the edifying; the turn. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the avoid foolish questions and genealogies and contentions and strivings about the law, the for they are unprofitable and vain, and the rejection of controversy. The verse thus records the rejection of fruitless controversy — teaching that Titus is to shun idle disputes about the law and turn to edifying truth.
10:
"A man that is an heretick after the first and second admonition reject;" The witnesses note the charge how to deal with a heretic. Gill notes a heretic, according to the notation of the word, is one who makes choice of an opinion on his own judgment, contrary to the generally received sense of the churches of Christ, obstinately persists in it, separates, forms a party, and sets himself at its head. The witnesses note Paul charging that a man who is a heretic, after the first and second admonition, be rejected; the orderly dealing with the obstinate heretic — that Paul gives the rule for the divisive errorist: "a man that is an heretick" — one who chooses and persists in error, breaking the church's unity — is not to be cut off rashly, but dealt with patiently first: "after the first and second admonition" (after he has been warned once and again, and refuses to repent) he is to be "reject[ed]" — shunned, put away, refused further fellowship — so that discipline is to be exercised, but only after due and patient warning, the obstinate alone being finally rejected; a man that is an heretick after the first and second admonition reject. The witnesses note a man that is an heretick. The witnesses note after the first and second admonition reject.

The witnesses note the orderly dealing with a heretic — after the first and second admonition, reject; discipline exercised, but only after due and patient warning.

So the witnesses make the verse the orderly dealing with the heretic. The lesson the expositors draw is that the obstinate heretic, after patient warning, is to be rejected. The witnesses note the heretic: "a man that is an heretick" — Gill notes one who persists in chosen error, breaking unity; the heretic; the chosen error; the persists; the breaks unity; the divisive. The expositors observe the forms a party: the heretic separates and forms a party; the forms a party; the separates; the a party; the head; the divides. The witnesses press the admonition: "after the first and second admonition" — after being warned once and again; the admonition; the warned; the once and again; the first and second; the patient. The expositors draw the lesson on the reject: "reject" — shunned, put away, refused fellowship; the reject; the shun; the put away; the no fellowship; the discipline. The witnesses observe the not rashly: he is not cut off rashly, but after due warning; the not rashly; the due warning; the patient; the after; the warned. The expositors note the obstinate alone: only the obstinate is finally rejected; the obstinate alone; the the obstinate; the finally; the rejected; the persists. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a man that is an heretick, the after the first and second admonition reject, and the dealing with the heretic. The verse thus records the orderly dealing with the heretic — teaching that the obstinate heretic, after patient warning, is to be rejected.
11:
"Knowing that he that is such is subverted, and sinneth, being condemned of himself." The witnesses note the heretic's self-condemned and ruined state. Gill notes that he that is such is "subverted" — overturned and demolished, like an edifice not only decaying but entirely everted, pulled down — so that there is no hope of recovery; he is in a desperate condition, having opposed the person, office, or sacrifice of Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that such a one is subverted, sinneth, and is condemned of himself; the heretic's ruin and self-conviction justifying his rejection — that Paul gives the reason the obstinate heretic may justly be rejected: "knowing that he that is such is subverted" (utterly overturned in the foundations of his faith, ruined past hope) "and sinneth" (persists wilfully in his sin) "being condemned of himself" — that is, self-condemned, for his own conscience convicts him, knowing the truth he forsakes, so that his rejection is no harshness, the man having pronounced sentence on himself by persisting against the light; being condemned of himself. The witnesses note he that is such is subverted, and sinneth. The witnesses note being condemned of himself.

The witnesses note the heretic's ruined, self-condemned state — subverted past hope, sinning wilfully, condemned by his own conscience; his rejection no harshness but just.

So the witnesses make the verse the heretic's self-conviction justifying rejection. The lesson the expositors draw is that the obstinate heretic is ruined and self-condemned, so his rejection is just. The witnesses note the subverted: "he that is such is subverted" — Gill notes overturned, ruined past hope; the subverted; the overturned; the ruined; the past hope; the demolished. The expositors observe the desperate: he is in a desperate condition, having opposed Christ; the desperate; the opposed Christ; the his person; the his sacrifice; the ruined. The witnesses press the sinneth: "and sinneth" — persists wilfully in his sin; the sinneth; the wilfully; the persists; the his sin; the obstinate. The expositors draw the lesson on the condemned of himself: "being condemned of himself" — self-condemned, his conscience convicts him; the condemned of himself; the self-condemned; the his conscience; the convicts; the knowing. The witnesses observe the against the light: he sins against the light he forsakes; the against the light; the the truth; the forsakes; the knowing; the wilful. The expositors note the rejection just: his rejection is no harshness but just; the rejection just; the no harshness; the just; the self-sentenced; the warranted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that is such is subverted and sinneth, the being condemned of himself, and the self-conviction. The verse thus records the heretic's self-conviction justifying rejection — teaching that the obstinate heretic is ruined and self-condemned, so his rejection is just.
12:
"When I shall send Artemas unto thee, or Tychicus, be diligent to come unto me to Nicopolis: for I have determined there to winter." The witnesses note Paul's plan to send a helper and summon Titus. Gill notes Artemas and Tychicus were both ministers of the gospel; there is no mention of Artemas elsewhere, while Tychicus is often spoken of as a faithful and beloved minister. The witnesses note Paul saying that when he sends Artemas or Tychicus, Titus should come to him at Nicopolis, where he had determined to winter; the personal arrangements for Titus to rejoin Paul — that Paul, turning to personal matters, lets Titus know his plans: he will send one of two trusted ministers, "Artemas... or Tychicus," to take up the work in Crete in Titus's place, and then Titus is to "be diligent to come" (make haste to come) to Paul at "Nicopolis," where Paul had "determined there to winter" — so that the work would not be left undone when Titus departed, the apostle providing a substitute and arranging the reunion; be diligent to come unto me to Nicopolis. The witnesses note when I shall send Artemas unto thee, or Tychicus. The witnesses note be diligent to come unto me to Nicopolis: for I have determined there to winter.

The witnesses note Paul's plan — to send Artemas or Tychicus to Crete and summon Titus to Nicopolis; a substitute provided so the work would not be left undone.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrangements for Titus to rejoin Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul provides a substitute for Crete and summons Titus to winter with him at Nicopolis. The witnesses note the send: "when I shall send Artemas unto thee, or Tychicus" — Gill notes both were gospel ministers; the send; the Artemas; the Tychicus; the ministers; the trusted. The expositors observe the Tychicus: Tychicus is a faithful and beloved minister; the Tychicus; the faithful; the beloved; the minister; the trusted. The witnesses press the come diligently: "be diligent to come unto me" — make haste to come to Paul; the come diligently; the make haste; the to Paul; the come; the diligent. The expositors draw the lesson on the Nicopolis: "to Nicopolis" — to Nicopolis, where Paul would winter; the Nicopolis; the the place; the winter; the the reunion; the come. The witnesses observe the winter: "for I have determined there to winter" — Paul determined to winter there; the winter; the determined; the there; the the plan; the stay. The expositors note the substitute: a substitute provided so the work not be left undone; the substitute; the the work; the not undone; the provided; the Crete. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when I shall send Artemas unto thee or Tychicus, the be diligent to come unto me to Nicopolis for I have determined there to winter, and the arrangements. The verse thus records the arrangements for Titus to rejoin Paul — teaching that Paul provides a substitute for Crete and summons Titus to winter with him at Nicopolis.
13:
"Bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently, that nothing be wanting unto them." The witnesses note the charge to help Zenas and Apollos on their way. Gill notes whether Zenas was brought up to the civil law among the Greeks or Romans is uncertain; it may be he was a Jewish lawyer or scribe, an interpreter of Moses's law, for among the Jews a lawyer and a scribe were one and the same. The witnesses note Paul charging Titus to bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently, that nothing be wanting to them; the duty of generous hospitality to travelling ministers — that Paul commends to Titus's care two servants of the gospel passing through, "Zenas the lawyer and Apollos" (the latter the eloquent and learned Apollos known elsewhere), charging that he "bring them on their journey diligently" (help them forward on their way with care and generosity) "that nothing be wanting unto them" (so that they lack nothing needful for the journey) — so that the believers are to provide liberally for the needs of travelling ministers of Christ; bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently. The witnesses note bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently. The witnesses note that nothing be wanting unto them.

The witnesses note the charge to help Zenas and Apollos on their way — that nothing be wanting; the duty of generous hospitality to travelling ministers of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the hospitality to travelling ministers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers are to provide liberally for the needs of travelling servants of the gospel. The witnesses note the Zenas: "bring Zenas the lawyer" — Gill notes Zenas a lawyer, perhaps a Jewish scribe; the Zenas; the the lawyer; the the scribe; the Moses's law; the interpreter. The expositors observe the Apollos: "and Apollos" — the eloquent and learned Apollos; the Apollos; the eloquent; the learned; the known; the the minister. The witnesses press the bring on their journey: "on their journey diligently" — help them forward with care and generosity; the bring on their journey; the help forward; the diligently; the care; the generosity. The expositors draw the lesson on the nothing wanting: "that nothing be wanting unto them" — that they lack nothing needful; the nothing wanting; the lack nothing; the needful; the the journey; the supplied. The witnesses observe the travelling ministers: provision for travelling ministers of Christ; the travelling ministers; the of Christ; the the gospel; the provide; the supplied. The expositors note the liberally: the believers provide liberally for their needs; the liberally; the provide; the their needs; the generous; the hospitality. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently, the that nothing be wanting unto them, and the hospitality to ministers. The verse thus records the hospitality to travelling ministers — teaching that the believers are to provide liberally for the needs of travelling servants of the gospel.
14:
"And let ours also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses, that they be not unfruitful." The witnesses note the charge that believers learn to maintain good works. Gill notes by "good works" are meant not only honest trades — though an honest, lawful employment is a good work to be maintained and followed — but the broader doing of good for necessary uses. The witnesses note Paul charging that believers also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses, that they be not unfruitful; the call for all the saints to be diligent in good and useful works — that Paul, from the case of helping Zenas and Apollos, draws a general lesson for all: "let ours also" (our people, all the believers) "learn to maintain good works" (give themselves to the practice of good — honest labour and works of charity) "for necessary uses" (to meet the real needs that arise, such as the relief of the saints and travelling ministers) — and that to a clear end, "that they be not unfruitful," that no believer's life be barren and useless, but every one fruitful in good; let ours also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses. The witnesses note let ours also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses. The witnesses note that they be not unfruitful.

The witnesses note the call for all believers to maintain good works for necessary uses — that none be unfruitful; every life diligent in good and useful works.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to fruitful good works. The lesson the expositors draw is that all believers are to give themselves to good and useful works, that none be barren. The witnesses note the ours also: "let ours also learn" — Gill notes all the believers, our people; the ours also; the our people; the all believers; the learn; the the saints. The expositors observe the maintain good works: "to maintain good works" — give themselves to good — labour and charity; the maintain good works; the honest labour; the charity; the good; the practise. The witnesses press the necessary uses: "for necessary uses" — to meet the real needs that arise; the necessary uses; the real needs; the relief; the ministers; the meet. The expositors draw the lesson on the not unfruitful: "that they be not unfruitful" — that no life be barren and useless; the not unfruitful; the not barren; the not useless; the fruitful; the every life. The witnesses observe the general lesson: from helping Zenas a general lesson for all; the general lesson; the from Zenas; the for all; the the saints; the diligent. The expositors note the fruitful in good: every believer is to be fruitful in good; the fruitful in good; the every believer; the fruitful; the good; the useful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let ours also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses, the that they be not unfruitful, and the call to good works. The verse thus records the call to fruitful good works — teaching that all believers are to give themselves to good and useful works, that none be barren.
15:
"All that are with me salute thee. Greet them that love us in the faith. Grace be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the closing salutations and benediction. Gill notes all the apostles, fellow labourers, and ministers of the gospel that were with Paul, and the members of the church where he was, sent their Christian salutation to Titus, who was greatly esteemed and whose praise was in all the churches. The witnesses note Paul sending greetings from all with him, bidding Titus greet them that love them in the faith, and closing with the benediction of grace; the parting salutations and blessing that close the epistle — that Paul ends with the warm exchange of Christian love: "all that are with me salute thee" (all his companions send their greetings to Titus, so esteemed among them), and he bids Titus in turn "greet them that love us in the faith" (the believers in Crete who love Paul and his fellows in the bond of the common faith) — and he seals the whole letter with his accustomed benediction, "Grace be with you all. Amen," invoking the free favour of God upon Titus and all the saints with him; Grace be with you all. Amen. The witnesses note all that are with me salute thee. Greet them that love us in the faith. The witnesses note Grace be with you all. Amen.

The witnesses note the closing salutations and benediction — greetings exchanged in the bond of the common faith, sealed with "Grace be with you all. Amen."

So the witnesses make the verse the parting salutations and blessing. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul closes with the exchange of Christian love and the benediction of grace upon all. The witnesses note the salute thee: "all that are with me salute thee" — Gill notes all Paul's companions greet the esteemed Titus; the salute thee; the all with me; the companions; the greet; the esteemed. The expositors observe the esteemed: Titus was greatly esteemed, his praise in all the churches; the esteemed; the his praise; the all the churches; the Titus; the loved. The witnesses press the greet them: "Greet them that love us in the faith" — greet the believers who love Paul in the faith; the greet them; the that love us; the in the faith; the the believers; the bond. The expositors draw the lesson on the common faith: in the bond of the common faith; the common faith; the the bond; the love; the believers; the faith. The witnesses observe the grace: "Grace be with you all" — the accustomed benediction of grace; the grace; the benediction; the be with you all; the blessing; the close. The expositors note the upon all: invoking God's free favour upon all the saints; the upon all; the God's favour; the all the saints; the blessing; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all that are with me salute thee greet them that love us in the faith, the Grace be with you all Amen, and the parting salutations. The verse thus records the parting salutations and blessing — teaching that Paul closes with the exchange of Christian love and the benediction of grace upon all.
